title: AI for Teachers: Practical Classroom Planning Workshop

learning_objectives: Explain common AI terms in plain teacher-friendly language.
Use AI practically for lesson planning, assessment, differentiation, feedback, classroom activities, student support, and teacher productivity.
Show live classroom-ready demos with reusable prompt patterns and clear expected outputs.
Apply responsible AI habits including verification, privacy protection, hallucination awareness, bias checks, and human review.
expected_outcomes: Teachers can explain commonly used AI terms without technical jargon.
Teachers leave with practical prompts, demo flows, and classroom-ready examples they can adapt immediately.
Teachers can identify when AI output should be edited, checked, or rejected before classroom use.

lesson.title: AI for Teachers: Practical Classroom Planning Workshop

blocks.title: Workshop Goal
blocks.content: This section shows teachers practical ways to use AI in everyday teaching work, with live demos, reusable prompts, and strong human review habits.

items.label: AI
items.description: Tools that can recognize patterns, assist decisions, or generate useful drafts.

items.label: Generative AI
items.description: AI that creates new text, summaries, worksheets, activities, or feedback drafts.

items.label: LLM
items.description: A large language model that predicts and generates text from the prompt you give it.

items.label: Prompt
items.description: The instruction you give the AI to shape the response.

items.label: Hallucination
items.description: An answer that sounds strong but is wrong, incomplete, or unsupported.

items.label: Workflow
items.description: A repeatable chain such as topic to lesson to activity to quiz to review.

items.label: Teacher-first use
items.description: Use AI to save time on planning, assessment drafting, differentiation, student support, and classroom preparation.

items.label: Demo-led learning
items.description: Open each demo to see what will be done, what output to expect, and what teachers can reuse.

items.label: Human review
items.description: Check accuracy, tone, fairness, alignment, and privacy before classroom use.

items.label: Routine productivity
items.description: Use AI for repetitive work such as summaries, messages, question banks, and first drafts.

blocks.title: What Teachers Can Use AI For

items.What AI Helps With: Draft objectives, structure lessons, suggest starters and exit tickets
items.Teacher Review: Check accuracy, pacing, and curriculum fit

items.What AI Helps With: Generate MCQs, short questions, answer keys, and rubrics
Rewrite for support, core, and extension levels
Turn a lecture topic into discussion, group work, debate, card sort, or station activity
Re-explain concepts, simplify language, generate examples, and create revision materials
items.Teacher Review: Check clarity, correctness, and age appropriateness

items.What AI Helps With: Draft comments, summarize notes, and prepare communication
items.Teacher Review: Check tone, privacy, and specific evidence

items.What AI Helps With: Remind teachers to verify claims and protect privacy
items.Teacher Review: Never skip human judgment on sensitive outputs

activity.title: Teacher Demo Studio
activity.instructions: Run one live prefilled teaching demo and narrate each stage from AI draft to teacher-approved classroom output.
activity.success_criteria: Teachers can follow one complete live demo, understand each stage, and see clearly where teacher review improves the result.

questions.prompt: What is the healthiest way for teachers to think about AI in this section?
options: As a replacement for teacher judgment
As a drafting and workflow assistant that still needs review
As a tool that is always factually correct
As a system that should receive all student data
questions.explanation: The teacher section treats AI as a practical assistant while teachers remain responsible for quality and safety.

questions.prompt: What should a teacher check before using AI-generated quiz questions?
options: Nothing if the output looks polished
Answer keys, wording, and alignment to the objective
Only whether the quiz is long enough
Whether the AI can explain the questions
questions.explanation: Assessment items must be reviewed for correctness and quality.

questions.prompt: Why should sensitive student information be handled carefully with AI tools?
options: Because privacy and policy risks still apply
Because AI tools cannot read text
Because AI only works with images
questions.explanation: Education work often involves confidential information, so privacy rules remain important.

reflection.prompt: What is one teaching task where AI could save you time, and what review steps would you keep before using the result?
reflection.placeholder: AI could help me draft a lesson plan, but I would still check accuracy, class fit, student level, and whether the questions match the objective.
